label: AWS Aurora Postgres
description: Connect to an Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL-compatible cluster using the writer or reader endpoint.

form.title: Connect AWS Aurora Postgres
form.subtitle: Enter your Aurora cluster endpoint, credentials, and SSL preference to connect.

methods.label: Cluster Endpoint (Password Auth)
methods.description: Paste your Aurora cluster writer or reader endpoint, port, database name, and credentials. The simplest path — no additional setup required beyond having network access to the cluster.
methods.how_to:
## Connect via Aurora Cluster Endpoint

### Find your endpoint in the AWS Console

1. Open the [AWS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/) and navigate to **RDS** (search for it in the top bar).
2. In the left sidebar click **Databases**.
3. Locate your Aurora cluster (type shows **Aurora** and engine shows **PostgreSQL-compatible**). Click the cluster identifier.
4. On the cluster detail page, scroll to the **Connectivity & security** tab.
5. You will see two endpoints:
   - **Writer endpoint** — routes to the current primary instance; use this for read-write workloads.
   - **Reader endpoint** — load-balances across read replicas; use this for read-only queries.
6. Copy the endpoint hostname (e.g. `my-cluster.cluster-abc123.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com`).
7. Note the **Port** (default `5432`) and the **DB name** you created when provisioning the cluster.

### Credentials

8. The master username and password were set at cluster creation. You can reset the master password from **Modify** on the cluster page if needed.
9. For least-privilege access, consider creating a dedicated PostgreSQL user: connect as master and run `CREATE USER anton WITH PASSWORD '...' LOGIN;` then `GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE mydb TO anton;`.

### Network access

10. Ensure Anton's host (your machine or the server running Anton) can reach the cluster. In **VPC security groups** attached to the cluster, confirm inbound TCP on port 5432 is allowed from your IP. For public clusters, check that **Publicly accessible** is set to **Yes** on the instance.

### SSL

11. Aurora Postgres supports and encourages SSL. Toggle **Require SSL** on unless your cluster explicitly disables it. Download the [AWS RDS CA bundle](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/UsingWithRDS.SSL.html) if your client needs certificate verification.

fields.name: host
fields.label: Cluster Endpoint Host
fields.description: Writer endpoint for read-write access, or reader endpoint for read-only. Found in RDS → Databases → your cluster → Connectivity & security tab.

fields.name: port
fields.label: Port
fields.description: Default Aurora Postgres port is 5432. Change only if your cluster uses a non-standard port.

fields.name: database
fields.label: Database Name
fields.description: The name of the specific PostgreSQL database to connect to within the cluster.

fields.name: user
fields.label: Database User
fields.description: Master user or a dedicated least-privilege user created in your Aurora cluster.

fields.name: password
fields.label: Password
fields.description: Password for the database user above. Set at cluster creation; reset via RDS → Modify if needed.

fields.name: ssl_enabled
fields.label: Require SSL
fields.description: Strongly recommended. Aurora Postgres supports SSL/TLS by default. Disable only if your cluster explicitly rejects SSL connections.

fields.name: endpoint_type
fields.label: Endpoint Type
fields.description: Indicates whether the host above is a writer or reader endpoint. Used for documentation and routing hints only — does not change the connection string.

options.label: Instance endpoint (direct to a specific node)

methods.label: IAM Database Authentication
methods.description: Generate a short-lived IAM auth token instead of a static password. Requires IAM DB auth enabled on the cluster and an IAM policy granting rds-db:connect. No long-lived passwords stored.
methods.how_to:
## IAM Database Authentication

Aurora Postgres supports IAM-based login: AWS generates a temporary token (valid 15 minutes) that replaces the password. Your IAM role or user must have the `rds-db:connect` permission.

### Enable IAM auth on the cluster

1. In the AWS Console go to **RDS → Databases** and click your Aurora cluster.
2. Click **Modify** → under **Database options** enable **IAM database authentication**.
3. Apply immediately or during the next maintenance window.

### Create an IAM policy

4. In **IAM → Policies → Create policy**, use this JSON:
   ```
   {
     "Effect": "Allow",
     "Action": "rds-db:connect",
     "Resource": "arn:aws:rds-db:REGION:ACCOUNT_ID:dbuser:CLUSTER_RESOURCE_ID/DB_USER"
   }
   ```
5. Attach the policy to the IAM user or role Anton will use.

### Create the DB user in Postgres

6. Connect to the cluster as master and run:
   `CREATE USER anton; GRANT rds_iam TO anton;`

### Configure Anton

7. Provide your AWS Access Key ID and Secret Access Key (or use an instance role if Anton runs on EC2).
8. Anton calls `rds:GenerateAuthToken` to produce a fresh token for each connection.

fields.name: host
fields.label: Cluster Endpoint Host
fields.description: Writer or reader endpoint from RDS → Databases → Connectivity & security tab.

fields.name: database
fields.label: Database Name
fields.description: The PostgreSQL database to connect to within the cluster.

fields.name: db_username
fields.label: Database User
fields.description: Must be a Postgres user granted the rds_iam role. Created with GRANT rds_iam TO <user>.

fields.name: aws_access_key_id
fields.label: AWS Access Key ID
fields.description: Access key for the IAM user that has the rds-db:connect permission. Leave blank to use the instance/environment role if Anton runs on AWS infrastructure.

fields.name: aws_secret_access_key
fields.label: AWS Secret Access Key
fields.description: Paired with the Access Key ID above. Stored encrypted locally by Anton.

fields.name: aws_region
fields.label: AWS Region
fields.description: The AWS region where your Aurora cluster is deployed (e.g. us-east-1, eu-west-1).

fields.name: ssl_enabled
fields.label: Require SSL
fields.description: IAM auth requires SSL — keep this enabled. Aurora will reject IAM token auth over non-SSL connections.
